result.markdown:
--- title: "Choose your columns with selectors" format: html: toc: true revealjs: slide-number: true toc: false code-fold: false code-tools: true --- ## Introduction to Selectors Complex column selection beyond simple name lists: ::: {#403bb0e9 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import skrub.selectors as s ``` ::: ## Selecting by Data Type Available selectors: - `.numeric()`: Numeric columns (int or float) - `.integer()`: Integer columns only - `.float()`: Floating-point columns - `.string()`: String columns - `.categorical()`: Categorical columns - `.any_date()`: Date or datetime columns - `.boolean()`: Boolean columns ## Example: Select String Columns ::: {#ec25860b .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} from skrub import SelectCols import pandas as pd df = pd.DataFrame({ "age": [25, 30, 35], "name": ["Alice", None, "Charlie"], "city": ["NYC", "LA", "Chicago"] }) SelectCols(cols=s.string()).fit_transform(df) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=2} ```{=html}
name city
0 Alice NYC
1 None LA
2 Charlie Chicago
``` ::: ::: ## Example: select/drop columns ::: {#690b9bda .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} df = pd.DataFrame({ "age": [25, 30, 35], "name": ["Alice", None, "Charlie"], "city": ["NYC", "LA", "Chicago"] }) selector = s.string() s.select(df, selector) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ```{=html}
name city
0 Alice NYC
1 None LA
2 Charlie Chicago
``` ::: ::: ## Selecting by Characteristics - `.all()`: All columns - `.has_nulls()`: Columns with at least one null - `.cardinality_below(threshold)`: Few unique values ::: {#0c3fa191 .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} df = pd.DataFrame({ "age": [25, 30, 35], "name": ["Alice", None, "Charlie"], "city": ["NYC", "LA", "Chicago"] }) SelectCols(cols=s.has_nulls()).fit_transform(df) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ```{=html}
name
0 Alice
1 None
2 Charlie
``` ::: ::: ## Selecting by Name - `.cols("name")`: Specific column name(s) - `.glob("pattern*")`: Unix shell-style globbing - `.regex("pattern")`: Regular expressions ::: {#cf0ad28a .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} df = pd.DataFrame({ "patient_id": [101, 102, 103], "metric_1": [0.3, 0.3, 0.6], "metric_2": [3, 5, 10], }) SelectCols(cols=s.glob("metric_*")).fit_transform(df) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=5} ```{=html}
metric_1 metric_2
0 0.3 3
1 0.3 5
2 0.6 10
``` ::: ::: ## Combining Selectors {.smaller} Use logical operators: ```{.python} # Inverse: NOT numeric ~s.numeric() # OR: datetime columns OR string columns s.any_date() | s.string() # AND: string columns without nulls s.string() & ~s.has_nulls() # XOR: either string, or name starts with "date_" but not both s.string() ^ s.glob("date_*") # Include: string columns and col "date" s.string() | "date" # Exclude: all columns except "datetime-col" s.all() - "datetime-col" ``` ::: {.notes} df = pd.DataFrame({"a": ["a", "b"], "date_1": [2024, 2025], "date_str": ["Monday", "Tuesday"]}) ::: ## Extracting Selected Columns Get the list of selected columns: ::: {#6a1fa8dc .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} df = pd.DataFrame( { "age": [25, 30, 35], "name": ["Alice", None, "Charlie"], "city": ["NYC", "LA", "Chicago"], } ) selector = s.has_nulls() columns_with_nulls = selector.expand(df) # Use in dataframe operations: capitalize columns with nulls df[columns_with_nulls].apply(lambda x : x.str.upper()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=6} ```{=html}
name
0 ALICE
1 None
2 CHARLIE
``` ::: ::: ## Custom Selectors: Filter by Condition ::: {#1e82bc38 .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} def more_nulls_than(col, threshold=0.5): return col.isnull().sum() / len(col) > threshold df = pd.DataFrame( { "no-nulls": [1, 2, 3, 4], "lotsa-nulls": [None, None, None, 4], } ) selector = s.filter(more_nulls_than, threshold=0.5) s.select(df, selector) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=7} ```{=html}
lotsa-nulls
0 NaN
1 NaN
2 NaN
3 4.0
``` ::: ::: ## Custom Selectors: Filter by Name ::: {#73b8c2c6 .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} df = pd.DataFrame( { "patient_id": [101, 102, 103], "metric_1": [0.3, 0.3, 0.6], "metric_2": [3, 5, 10], } ) selector = s.filter_names(lambda name: name.startswith("metric_")) s.select(df, selector) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=8} ```{=html}
metric_1 metric_2
0 0.3 3
1 0.3 5
2 0.6 10
``` ::: ::: ## What we have seen in this chapters - Use selectors for flexible, reusable column selection - Combine selectors with logical operators - `expand()` extracts selected column names - Create custom selectors for domain-specific logic - Perfect with `ApplyToCols`, `SelectCols`, `DropCols`
